title: GIFT City IFSC and Foreign Currency Settlement System
focus: Understanding the FCSS, its operator, and the regulatory framework of GIFT City IFSC under IFSCA.
rationale: Distinct topic focusing on India's first IFSC, its regulatory body (IFSCA), and the newly launched FCSS.
sectionExcerpt: Ministry of Finance launched the Foreign Currency Settlement System (FCSS) at GIFT IFSC. It is authorized by IFSCA under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007, to settle foreign currency transactions locally. CCIL IFSC Limited operates it. GIFT City is India's first and only IFSC, established as an SEZ in 2015 and designated as a non-resident zone under FEMA. IFSCA (established 2020) regulates it.
evidenceBullets: FCSS enables local settlement of foreign currency transactions between IFSC Banking Units.
CCIL IFSC Limited is the operator of the FCSS.
GIFT City is India's first and only IFSC, established as an SEZ in 2015.
Designated as a non-resident zone under FEMA.
IFSCA was established in 2020 under the IFSCA Act, 2019 to regulate financial products and institutions in IFSCs.

title: Bond Yield Dynamics and Interest Rates
focus: The inverse relationship between bank interest rates, bond prices, and bond yields.
rationale: A core macroeconomic concept explaining how bond prices and yields react to changes in bank interest rates.
sectionExcerpt: The bond market operates on an equilibrium where bank interest rates inversely affect bond prices. When bank interest rates rise, existing fixed-coupon bond prices fall, causing their effective yield to rise. Conversely, when bank interest rates fall, existing bond prices rise, resulting in a lower yield.
evidenceBullets: When bank interest rates rise, bond prices fall and bond yields rise.
When bank interest rates fall, bond prices rise and bond yields fall.
Higher interest rates make existing bonds with fixed lower coupons less desirable, dropping their price.

title: Specialized Bonds: Transition and Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds
focus: Characteristics and objectives of Transition Bonds and Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds.
rationale: Focuses on specific bond instruments, their regulatory definitions, and their purposes (decarbonization vs. capital adequacy).
sectionExcerpt: Transition Bonds are a sub-category of green debt securities under SEBI, aimed at helping hard-to-abate sectors like steel and cement raise capital for decarbonization. Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds, or perpetual bonds, are issued by banks without a maturity date but include a call option. They are used by banks to meet capital adequacy requirements.
evidenceBullets: Transition Bonds are a sub-category of green debt securities as per SEBI regulations.
Transition Bonds help hard-to-abate sectors raise capital for progressive decarbonization.
Additional Tier 1 (AT1) Bonds are perpetual bonds issued by banks without a maturity date.
AT1 bonds include a call option and are issued to meet capital adequacy requirements.

title: Payments Regulatory Board and Payment Systems Act
focus: Composition, tenure, and functions of the Payments Regulatory Board under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.
rationale: A distinct regulatory and statutory topic detailing the RBI's Payments Regulatory Board structure and the governing 2007 Act.
sectionExcerpt: The RBI constituted the Payments Regulatory Board under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007. The Board consists of the RBI Governor (ex officio Chairperson), Deputy Governor, one RBI officer, and three central government nominees. Members have a 4-year tenure. The Act regulates payment systems in India, with RBI as the central overseeing authority.
evidenceBullets: The Payments Regulatory Board is constituted under the Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007.
The RBI Governor serves as the ex officio Chairperson of the Board.
The Board includes three central government nominated experts with a 4-year tenure.
The Payment and Settlement Systems Act, 2007 designates RBI as the central authority overseeing payment infrastructure in India.
